description_long: This object is a traditional Japanese torii gate, typically found at the entrance to Shinto shrines. It features two robust cylindrical vertical pillars supporting a pair of horizontal crossbars, with the uppermost bar slightly curved and painted black. The main structure is painted a weathered red, with the lower portions of the pillars darkened, possibly to simulate wear or protective coating. The surface shows signs of age and exposure, with visible texture and some discoloration. The gate is simple in design, lacking intricate ornamentation, and stands as a symbolic threshold between the secular and sacred. Its proportions and materials evoke a sense of historical and cultural significance.
description: A weathered red Japanese torii gate with black-tipped pillars and a curved black top crossbar, symbolizing a shrine entrance.
description_view_0: Front view showing both red pillars, the horizontal crossbars, and the curved black top bar.
description_view_1: Side view revealing the thickness of the crossbars and a single pillar, with minimal visible detail.
description_view_2: Rear view similar to the front, showing both pillars and the crossbars, with less surface detail visible.
description_view_3: Opposite side view, again showing the thickness of the crossbars and a single pillar.
